narrative:
As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.70—well above the typical 3.21 average—and an LSAT score of 37, which edges out the average of 36.70, you've built an impressive academic foundation that many law school hopefuls would envy. Your family comes from an upper-middle income bracket, and you're planning to attend full-time, aligning with the vast majority of students at 92.7%. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your probability of failure at 45.9%. This threshold triggered the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes candidates predicted to pass with high confidence to maintain their bar passage rates.

The model's decision hinged on just a few key factors from your profile, with your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1—far below the average of 5.53—standing out as the most influential. This low ranking in your third-year grades strongly pushed the prediction toward expecting bar exam struggles, outweighing your other strengths. Next in importance was your undergraduate GPA, which had a counterbalancing effect; being notably higher than average, it pulled the prediction toward success and passing the bar. Your LSAT score played a similar supportive role as the third-biggest factor, nudging things positively because it exceeded the norm, signaling good potential.

Compared to typical students, your early-year decile of 1 matches your third-year performance, suggesting a consistent challenge in school rankings that set you apart from the pack, while your GPA and LSAT shine brighter than most. It's understandable to feel frustrated, especially with such strong metrics in key areas, but this highlights how recent academic decile performance can overshadow them in bar passage predictions. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your school deciles, perhaps through targeted tutoring or additional undergrad-level work if possible. You might also seek feedback from advisors on bolstering weaker areas or applying to schools with more holistic reviews. With your GPA and LSAT as assets, persistence could turn this around—many applicants improve their profiles and succeed on later attempts.
